ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general rice noodle roll / cheung fun concept; no specific tiger prawn and bamboo shoot variant exists. Closest parent used.
ontology.foodex2_description: Crustaceans and products thereof — steamed prawn dim sum preparation
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a specific cheung fun with tiger prawn and bamboo shoot entry. A0CKP (crustacean-based steamed product) used as closest parent.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No suitable SNOMED CT concept exists for this specific dim sum preparation.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical dim sum serving of fresh tiger prawn with young bamboo shoot rice roll consists of approximately 3 rolls weighing around 180 g in total, served with light soy sauce and sesame oil on the side.

nutrient_source.title: Cheung Fun (Rice Noodle Roll) with Prawn — composite estimate from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy: Crustaceans, shrimp, mixed species, cooked, moist heat (NDB 15152); Rice flour, white (NDB 20070); Bamboo shoots, cooked, boiled, drained, without salt (NDB 11028); Sesame oil (NDB 04058)
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central (SR Legacy)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single approved-source entry exists for tiger prawn cheung fun with bamboo shoot. Values are estimated from a weighted composite of USDA SR Legacy entries for cooked shrimp (~35% by weight), rice flour noodle sheet (~50% by weight), young bamboo shoot (~10% by weight), and sesame oil/soy sauce (~5% by weight). Proportions are based on standard Cantonese cheung fun construction. Energy cross-checked against FAO/INFOODS SEA regional analogues for steamed prawn dim sum.

gi_source.title: Rice noodle (steamed) — GI analogous estimate; reference: Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values: 2008. Diabetes Care 2008;31(12):2281-2283.
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care peer-reviewed journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for tiger prawn cheung fun with bamboo shoot. GI estimated at 40 based on steamed rice noodle (cheung fun) analogues reported in the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care), which list steamed rice noodle products in the GI range 38–45. The high protein content from tiger prawn (~35% of dish weight) further attenuates glycemic response. GL per serving calculated as: (40 × 13.5 g carbohydrate per 100 g × 180 g serving) / 100 = 9.72, rounded to 9.7.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content (~310 mg per 100 g; ~558 mg per 180 g serving) from soy sauce and natural prawn sodium
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size to one roll and request reduced soy sauce, as a full 3-roll serving provides approximately 558 mg sodium, representing roughly 24% of the 2,300 mg daily limit recommended by the American Heart Association.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycemic index (~40) and moderate carbohydrate content attenuated by high protein from tiger prawn
type2_diabetes.guidance: With a GI of approximately 40 and a glycemic load of ~9.7 per serving, this dish is suitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes when consumed in standard portions, as the high-protein tiger prawn content slows gastric emptying and blunts postprandial glucose rise; however, sodium from soy sauce should be monitored.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Cholesterol from tiger prawn (~72 mg per 100 g) and sodium from soy sauce; low saturated fat is a positive factor
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Tiger prawn is relatively low in saturated fat (0.5 g per 100 g) and provides beneficial omega-3 fatty acids, but the cholesterol content (~130 mg per serving) and sodium load (~558 mg per serving) warrant moderation for individuals with established cardiovascular disease or dyslipidaemia.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Fresh Tiger Prawn with Young Bamboo Shoot Rice Roll is a Cantonese dim sum dish consisting of whole or halved tiger prawns and tender young bamboo shoot strips encased in a thin, silky steamed rice noodle sheet (cheung fun). The dish originates from Guangdong province, China, and is widely served in Cantonese dim sum restaurants across Singapore, Hong Kong, and the broader Chinese diaspora. The rice noodle sheet is made from a batter of rice flour and water, steamed on a flat tray until translucent and delicate, then rolled around the prawn and bamboo shoot filling. The result is a soft, smooth, slightly chewy roll with a sweet, briny flavour from the tiger prawn and a mild, earthy crunch from the bamboo shoot. It is typically served with a drizzle of light soy sauce and sesame oil, and sometimes a touch of oyster sauce. Nutritionally, the dish provides moderate energy (~112 kcal per 100 g), a meaningful protein contribution from tiger prawn (~8.2 g per 100 g), and a low glycemic index (~40) due to the steamed rice noodle base and the protein-attenuated glycemic response. Saturated fat is low (0.5 g per 100 g), making it a relatively heart-friendly dim sum option. Sodium is moderate (~310 mg per 100 g) primarily from soy sauce, which is a consideration for individuals managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The dish is dairy-free and suitable for pescatarian diets. It contains crustacean and soy allergens. In Singapore, it is commonly ordered as part of a shared dim sum spread during yum cha (morning tea) and is available at Cantonese dim sum restaurants including Loong Dim Sum on North Canal Road.

confidence_reason: No single approved-source entry exists for this specific dish (tiger prawn cheung fun with bamboo shoot). Nutrient values are estimated from a weighted composite of USDA SR Legacy entries for the component ingredients (cooked shrimp, rice flour, bamboo shoot, sesame oil). GI is estimated from steamed rice noodle analogues in the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2008. Confidence is medium rather than low because the component-level USDA data is high quality and the dish construction is well-defined.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are composite estimates from USDA SR Legacy component ingredients; no direct HPB Singapore Foodo or ICMR-NIN entry was found for this specific dish. 2. GI of 40 is an analogous estimate based on steamed rice noodle (cheung fun) data from the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care 31:2281-2283); no direct GI measurement for tiger prawn cheung fun with bamboo shoot was located. 3. vitamin_d_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA SR Legacy entries for the component ingredients at the level of precision required for this composite. 4. trans_fat_g is reported as 0.0; confirmed as negligible/trace in USDA SR Legacy for all component ingredients used. 5. Halal status is marked false because the soy sauce used in restaurant preparation may contain non-halal additives; consumers should verify with the specific restaurant. 6. The dish is served at Loong Dim Sum, North Canal Road, Singapore, at SGD 12.50 per order. 7. Sodium estimate includes contribution from light soy sauce drizzle (~50 mg per 100 g) added to the composite base.

improvement_actions_taken: Constructed full multilingual local_names for Singaporean Chinese cuisine (Chinese simplified, traditional, Malay, Tamil, Hindi).
Built composite nutrient estimate from USDA SR Legacy component ingredients with documented weight proportions.
Estimated GI at 40 from steamed rice noodle analogues in University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2008.
Documented vitamin_d_mcg = -1 sentinel value with explicit justification in notes.
Verified aliases_by_language and flat aliases list for perfect consistency.
Applied GI category threshold rule: GI 40 ≤ 55 → gi_category = 'low'.
Set ontology match types to only allowed values: 'closest_parent' and 'none'.
Included specific clinical guidance for all three required health conditions.
